unitTitle: KS2 Y3 Events and actions in programs (Teach Computing — adapted)

lessons.title: Moving a sprite
lessons.objectives:
I can explain that an event makes an action happen
I can name the event and the action in a block stack
I can choose sensible keys to move a sprite and say why
lessons.outline:
Routine first (same every lesson). Vocabulary on board: event · action · sprite · key press · move. TA: everyone has the starter worksheet; the shared Scratch project open. Read the 3 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–5) Starter — how do games control movement? Steer to keys on the keyboard; show the arrow-keys photo on the starter worksheet.
2. (5–15) I-do → we-do — event and action. Point at the yellow block (event = key press) and the blue blocks (action = point and move). Pupils SORT blocks into Event vs Action on the starter worksheet. Likely error: thinking the sprite moves on its own → fix-words “nothing happens until the event.”
3. (15–30) Explore the project — press the keys and watch one sprite move. Pupils LABEL the event and action on the activity worksheet.
4. (30–50) You-do — choose your own keys. • Support: choose the up key only. • Core: choose all four arrow keys. • Challenge: explain why arrow keys are clearer than random letters. TA: prompt, do not do it for them. Show-your-work: project link + screenshot.
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the 3 “I can…”; each pupil names one event and one action on their screen.
Differentiation on the SAME task, low floor / high ceiling. No flashing/sound on slides; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Maze movement
lessons.objectives:
I can choose and resize a character for my maze
I can program a sprite to move in four directions
I can copy a block stack and change it
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: sprite · resize · move · point in direction · algorithm. TA: starter worksheet out; the maze Scratch project open. Read the 3 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — the maze. Show the Start→Finish maze; ask if a big sprite fits the paths. Pupils answer the resize question on the starter worksheet.
2. (10–20) I-do → we-do — choose and resize a sprite. Model changing the Size value and pressing Enter. Likely error: a sprite too big for the paths → fix-words “make the Size number smaller, then press Enter.”
3. (20–40) We-do → you-do — program one direction, then copy it. Build the “right” stack (event → point in direction 90 → move 10). Pupils ORDER the build steps on the activity worksheet, then duplicate and change the copies (up 0, right 90, down 180, left -90). • Support: up only. • Core: all four. • Challenge: change the steps value to move faster/slower. Show-your-work: project link + screenshot.
4. (40–50) You-do — test in the maze; debug if a direction is wrong.
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the 3 “I can…”; each pupil shows one direction working.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Drawing lines
lessons.objectives:
I can use a programming extension (Pen)
I can use pen down to draw a line
I can choose blocks to set up my program
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: extension block · pen down · pen up · set up. TA: starter worksheet out; a movement project open; the Setup worksheet ready. Read the 3 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — extension blocks. Show where Add Extension is and choose Pen. Pupils answer the starter worksheet questions.
2. (10–20) I-do → we-do — pen down draws a line. Model adding pen down, then move the sprite. Likely error: “pen down” read as putting a pen on the table → fix-words “pen down means the pen draws.”
3. (20–40) Set up the program (initialisation). Show the setup blocks (when green flag clicked → go to x:0 y:0 → set pen colour → erase all). Pupils ORDER the setup on the activity worksheet, then add it to their project. • Support: copy the setup as shown. • Core: choose your own pen colour. • Challenge: explain why setup goes under the green flag. Show-your-work: project link + screenshot.
4. (40–50) You-do — draw a picture with the pen; check it starts the same every run.
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the 3 “I can…”; each pupil shows one drawn line.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

resources.title: Program setup blocks (Teach Computing).png
resources.sourceAttribution: Teach Computing Curriculum © Raspberry Pi Foundation, OGL v3.0.

lessons.title: Adding features
lessons.objectives:
I can name extra Pen blocks and say what they do
I can choose a key to turn a feature on
I can join the event and action to make a feature work
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: pen up · change pen colour · change pen size · event · action. TA: starter worksheet out; the experimenting Pen project open. Read the 3 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — the Pen blocks. Name set pen colour, change pen size by 1/-1, pen up, pen down, erase all. Pupils MATCH each block to what it does on the starter worksheet. Likely error: mixing up pen up with erase all → fix-words “pen up stops drawing; erase all clears the screen.”
2. (10–20) We-do — experiment with the blocks; press u (pen up) and d (pen down) and watch the line stop and start.
3. (20–35) You-do — add a feature. Each pupil joins a Pen block to a key (event → action). Plan it on the activity worksheet first. • Support: add pen up on the u key. • Core: add a colour or size change. • Challenge: add two features and explain the key choices. Show-your-work: project link + screenshot.
4. (35–50) Test the features; debug if a key does the wrong thing.
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the 3 “I can…”; each pupil shows one feature and names its key.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Debugging movement
lessons.objectives:
I can test a program against a design
I can match a piece of code to what it does
I can find and fix a bug in a program
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: bug · debug · error · design · test. TA: starter worksheet out; the debugging Scratch project open. Read the 3 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — what is a bug? Name the parts of the Scratch screen; pupils LABEL the green flag, stop button, stage and instructions on the starter worksheet.
2. (10–20) I-do — test against the design. Compare “should happen” with “does happen”; a mismatch is a bug.
3. (20–40) We-do → you-do — find and fix the bug. The left arrow makes the sprite go right (point in direction 90). Pupils answer the activity worksheet (is it a bug? which block? what should the value be → -90), then fix it. Likely error: blaming the event block → fix-words “the event is fine; the action has the wrong number.” • Support: change the one number as shown. • Core: find and fix it in their own project. • Challenge: fix a second bug (a key with pen down where it should be pen up). Show-your-work: project link + screenshot.
4. (40–50) Test the fixes against the design again.
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the 3 “I can…”; each pupil names one bug they fixed.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Making a project
lessons.objectives:
I can make design choices and say why
I can build my design into a maze project
I can test and evaluate my project
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: design · code · set up · test · debug · event · action. TA: starter worksheet out; the template Scratch project open; the design worksheet ready. Read the 3 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — design first. Show the blank maze; pupils ORDER the five build steps on the starter worksheet.
2. (10–20) I-do → we-do — design it. Choose sprite, keys (events) and pen colour; fill the plan rows on the activity worksheet.
3. (20–40) You-do — build it in five steps: add/resize/recentre the sprite → choose event blocks → add motion blocks → add pen blocks → set up (x: -200 y: 100). Likely error: forgetting the minus sign in x: -200 → fix-words “the start needs a minus sign for x.” • Support: follow a part-completed design. • Core: full own design. • Challenge: add an extra feature. Show-your-work: project link + screenshot.
4. (40–50) Test it against the design and debug; then evaluate (what works / what to improve) on the activity worksheet.
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the 3 “I can…”; each pupil shows their project and names one thing that works well.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.
